title: The Laplace Transform
subtitle: Foundations, Toolkit, Solving ODEs, Convolution, Advanced Applications, and Master Tables
description: The Laplace transform converts a differential equation in $t$ into an algebraic equation in $s$. Initial data is baked in from the start, discontinuous and impulsive forcing become ordinary algebra, and the structure of a system shows up as poles in the complex plane. The entire unit is one pipeline: transform, solve the algebra, invert.

atAGlance.title: Unit 12 at a Glance: The Laplace Transform
atAGlance.summary: The Laplace transform converts a differential equation in $t$ into an algebraic equation in $s$. Initial data is baked in from the start, discontinuous and impulsive forcing become ordinary algebra, and the structure of a system shows up as poles in the complex plane. The entire unit is one pipeline: transform, solve the algebra, invert.
bullets: \textbf{Foundations} --- the defining integral, existence (exponential order), the Euler trick, and the $t=0$ initial-condition traps.
\textbf{The toolkit} --- linearity, the two shift theorems, Heaviside and Dirac packaging, and the transforms of derivatives and integrals.
\textbf{Solving ODEs} --- the five-step method, partial fractions, discontinuous and impulse forcing, systems, and periodic inputs.
\textbf{Convolution} --- the convolution theorem, Duhamel's principle, Volterra integral equations, transfer functions, and stability.
\textbf{Advanced} --- PDEs by Laplace, Abel's integral equation, and resonance read off the $s$-plane.
\textbf{Reference} --- the master transform and property tables, the inversion playbook, and the initial/final-value (steady-state) theorems.

cheatSubtitle: Condensed Cheat Sheet: Transforms, Inversion, Discontinuous Forcing, and the $s$-Plane

cheatTakeaway.title: Unit Key Takeaway
cheatTakeaway.body:
The Laplace transform is one disciplined pipeline:
\textcolor{accentorange}{transform $\to$ solve the algebra $\to$ invert}. Initial data
rides in through the derivative rules, discontinuities become $e^{-cs}$ and $\delta$
bookkeeping, products become convolutions, and the
\textcolor{accentorange}{geometry of the poles} tells you whether a system decays,
oscillates, or blows up before you invert a single term.

modules.title: Intuition and Motivation
modules.topics: What the transform does and why: the frequency-domain picture, poles as the fingerprint of a signal, and the trade of calculus in $t$ for algebra in $s$.

formulas.title: The Transform as an Operator
formulas.body:
The Laplace transform takes a function $f(t)$ defined for $t \ge 0$ and produces a
function $F(s)$ of a new variable $s$. It is an \emph{integral operator}: it acts on
a whole function and returns a whole function. Reading $F(s)$ as a function of $s$ is
reading $f$ in the \textcolor{accentorange}{frequency domain}: each value of $s$ asks ``how much of
the mode $e^{st}$ is inside $f$?'' Sharp features and oscillations in $t$ become poles
and resonances in $s$. The transform is a change of coordinates that makes the
\emph{structure} of a signal --- its decay rates and frequencies --- visible as the
\textcolor{accentorange}{location of poles} in the complex plane.

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body:
The transform is a machine that trades \textcolor{accentorange}{calculus in $t$}
for \textcolor{accentorange}{algebra in $s$}. Differentiation becomes multiplication
by $s$, integration becomes division by $s$, and that is the entire reason the method
solves differential equations.

quiz.prompt: The Laplace transform of $f(t)$ is defined by which integral?
The Laplace transform converts a function of $t$ into a function of which variable?
Euler's formula expresses $e^{i\theta}$ as which combination?
In the complex exponential $e^{st}$ with $s = \sigma + i\omega$, what does the imaginary part $\omega$ control?
Conceptually, the Laplace transform decomposes a signal into a combination of which building blocks?
In the $s$-domain, what do the pole locations of a system's transform tell you?
quiz.answer: The system's natural frequencies and growth or decay rates

modules.title: Definition and Computing Transforms
modules.topics: The defining integral and its decaying kernel $e^{-st}$, the base transforms $\mathcal{L}\{1\}$ and $\mathcal{L}\{e^{at}\}$ computed from the definition, and the master transform table.

formulas.title: The Defining Integral
formulas.body:
\[ \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = F(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st} f(t)\,dt. \]
The integral runs over $[0,\infty)$, so the transform sees only the future
($t \ge 0$). The kernel $e^{-st}$ is a decaying weight (for $\operatorname{Re}(s)>0$)
that tames the growth of $f$ and guarantees the integral converges in a half-plane.

formulas.title: The Base Transforms, Straight from the Definition
formulas.body:
For $f(t)=1$:
\[ \mathcal{L}\{1\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st}\,dt
   = \textcolor{workpurple}{\left[ -\tfrac{1}{s} e^{-st} \right]_{0}^{\infty}}
   = \textcolor{workpurple}{0 - \left(-\tfrac{1}{s}\right)} = \frac{1}{s}, \quad s>0. \]
For $f(t)=e^{at}$ the two exponentials merge:
\[ \mathcal{L}\{e^{at}\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s-a)t}\,dt
   = \textcolor{workpurple}{\left[ -\tfrac{1}{s-a} e^{-(s-a)t} \right]_{0}^{\infty}}
   = \frac{1}{s-a}, \quad s>a. \]

formulas.title: Master Transform Table
formulas.body:
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5}
\[
\begin{array}{ll|ll}
f(t) & F(s) & f(t) & F(s) \\ \hline
1 & \dfrac{1}{s} & e^{at} & \dfrac{1}{s-a} \\
t^n & \dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}} & t^{a}\;(a>-1) & \dfrac{\Gamma(a+1)}{s^{a+1}} \\
\sin\omega t & \dfrac{\omega}{s^2+\omega^2} & \cos\omega t & \dfrac{s}{s^2+\omega^2} \\
\sinh\omega t & \dfrac{\omega}{s^2-\omega^2} & \cosh\omega t & \dfrac{s}{s^2-\omega^2} \\
e^{at}\sin\omega t & \dfrac{\omega}{(s-a)^2+\omega^2} & e^{at}\cos\omega t & \dfrac{s-a}{(s-a)^2+\omega^2} \\
t\sin\omega t & \dfrac{2\omega s}{(s^2+\omega^2)^2} & t\cos\omega t & \dfrac{s^2-\omega^2}{(s^2+\omega^2)^2} \\
u(t-c) & \dfrac{e^{-cs}}{s} & \delta(t-c) & e^{-cs} \\
\end{array}
\]

examples.title: Worked Example --- $\mathcal{L}\{t\}$ from the Definition
examples.setup:
The power rule $\mathcal{L}\{t^n\}=n!/s^{n+1}$ is itself a consequence of the
definition. Integrate $\int_0^\infty t\,e^{-st}\,dt$ by parts with $u=t$,
$dv=e^{-st}dt$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\left[ -\frac{t}{s}e^{-st}\right]_0^\infty
   + \frac{1}{s}\int_0^\infty e^{-st}\,dt}
   = \textcolor{workpurple}{0 + \frac{1}{s}\cdot\frac{1}{s}} = \frac{1}{s^2}. \]
The boundary term vanishes because $e^{-st}$ kills the linear growth at infinity.
Repeating the integration by parts $n$ times produces the factorial and the
$s^{n+1}$ in the denominator.

quiz.prompt: Compute the Laplace transform of $f(t) = 1$.
Compute the Laplace transform of $f(t) = e^{at}$.
What is the general formula for the Laplace transform of $t^n$?
Compute the Laplace transform of $t^3$.

modules.title: Properties, Linearity, Existence, and Inverses
modules.topics: Linearity of the transform and its inverse, sufficient conditions for existence (piecewise continuity and exponential order), the first shift theorem, the Euler trick for trig, and the master operational table.

formulas.title: Linearity
formulas.body:
\[ \mathcal{L}\{a f(t) + b g(t)\} = a\,\mathcal{L}\{f\} + b\,\mathcal{L}\{g\}. \]
The same holds for the inverse: $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{aF+bG\} = af + bg$. Linearity is
what lets us break any input into a sum of known pieces.

formulas.title: Sufficient Conditions for Existence
formulas.body:
If $f$ is \textbf{piecewise continuous} on $[0,\infty)$ and of \textbf{exponential
order} --- meaning there exist constants $M>0$, $a$, and $T$ with
\[ |f(t)| \le M e^{at} \quad \text{for all } t \ge T, \]
then $F(s)$ exists for all $s > a$, and $F(s)\to 0$ as $s\to\infty$.

formulas.title: First Shift Theorem ($s$-Shift)
formulas.body:
\[ \mathcal{L}\{e^{at} f(t)\} = F(s-a). \]
Equivalently, $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s-a)\} = e^{at} f(t)$: multiplying $f(t)$ by
$e^{at}$ in the time domain \emph{shifts the variable} in the $s$-domain.

formulas.title: Master Properties / Operational Table
formulas.body:
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.6}
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\text{Linearity} & \mathcal{L}\{af+bg\} = aF+bG \\
\text{First shift } (s) & \mathcal{L}\{e^{at}f(t)\} = F(s-a) \\
\text{Second shift } (t) & \mathcal{L}\{u(t-c)f(t-c)\} = e^{-cs}F(s) \\
\text{Derivative} & \mathcal{L}\{f^{(n)}\} = s^n F - s^{n-1}f(0)-\cdots-f^{(n-1)}(0) \\
\text{Integral} & \mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t f\right\} = F(s)/s \\
\text{Multiply by } t^n & \mathcal{L}\{t^n f\} = (-1)^n F^{(n)}(s) \\
\text{Divide by } t & \mathcal{L}\{f/t\} = \int_s^\infty F(\sigma)\,d\sigma \\
\text{Convolution} & \mathcal{L}\{f*g\} = F(s)G(s) \\
\text{Periodic } (T) & \mathcal{L}\{f\} = \dfrac{1}{1-e^{-sT}}\int_0^T e^{-st}f\,dt \\
\end{array}
\]

examples.title: Worked Example --- A Damped Cosine

methodology.title: Method --- Transforming Trig via $e^{i\omega t}$
steps:
Transform it as an ordinary exponential with $a = i\omega$:
\[ \mathcal{L}\{e^{i\omega t}\} = \frac{1}{s - i\omega}. \]

Match real and imaginary parts:
\[ \mathcal{L}\{\cos\omega t\} = \frac{s}{s^2+\omega^2}, \qquad
   \mathcal{L}\{\sin\omega t\} = \frac{\omega}{s^2+\omega^2}. \]

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Functions That Grow Too Fast
warning.body:
$f(t)=e^{t^2}$ is \emph{not} of exponential order --- it outruns every $Me^{at}$ ---
so its Laplace transform does not exist. By contrast every polynomial, every
$e^{at}$, every $\sin$ and $\cos$, and any product of these is of exponential order.
A quick existence test: if $\lim_{s\to\infty}F(s)\ne 0$, the candidate $F$ cannot be
a Laplace transform of a well-behaved $f$.

quiz.prompt: The linearity of the Laplace transform states that $\mathcal{L}\{a f(t) + b g(t)\}$ equals what?
Which conditions guarantee that the Laplace transform of $f$ exists?
quiz.answer: $f$ is piecewise continuous and of exponential order

quiz.prompt: The proof that the Laplace transform is linear rests on which underlying property?
For a constant $c$, what does $\mathcal{L}\{c f(t)\}$ equal?

modules.title: Transforms of Derivatives and Integrals
modules.topics: The derivative rules that power the whole method, the integral rule, multiplication and division by $t$, and the one-sided-limit traps at $t=0$.

formulas.title: Derivative Rules
formulas.body:
\[ \mathcal{L}\{f'\} = sF(s) - f(0), \]
\[ \mathcal{L}\{f''\} = s^2 F(s) - s f(0) - f'(0), \]
\[ \mathcal{L}\{f^{(n)}\} = s^n F(s) - s^{n-1} f(0) - s^{n-2} f'(0) - \cdots - f^{(n-1)}(0). \]
The pattern: each derivative contributes one power of $s$ and peels off one initial
value, highest-order data attached to the highest power of $s$.

formulas.title: Integral and Weight Rules
formulas.body:
\[ \mathcal{L}\!\left\{ \int_0^t f(\tau)\,d\tau \right\} = \frac{F(s)}{s}, \qquad
   \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{F(s)}{s}\right\} = \int_0^t f(\tau)\,d\tau. \]
\[ \mathcal{L}\{t^n f(t)\} = (-1)^n \frac{d^n F}{ds^n}, \qquad
   \mathcal{L}\!\left\{\frac{f(t)}{t}\right\} = \int_s^\infty F(\sigma)\,d\sigma. \]

examples.title: Worked Example --- Multiplication by $t$
examples.setup:
Find $\mathcal{L}\{t\sin\omega t\}$. With $F(s)=\dfrac{\omega}{s^2+\omega^2}$,
apply $\mathcal{L}\{t f(t)\} = -F'(s)$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{-\frac{d}{ds}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{s^2+\omega^2}\right)
   = -\omega \cdot \frac{-2s}{(s^2+\omega^2)^2}
   = \frac{2\omega s}{(s^2+\omega^2)^2}.} \]
The squared denominator is the algebraic signature of \emph{resonance} --- a
repeated complex pole.

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body:
The transform method is an \textcolor{accentorange}{initial-value} method by
construction: the data $f(0), f'(0), \dots$ enters the algebra immediately through
the derivative rules, so there is never a separate ``apply the initial conditions''
step at the end the way there is with undetermined coefficients.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Which Zero?
warning.body:
In $\mathcal{L}\{f'\} = sF(s) - f(0)$ the term $f(0)$ is the initial value
\emph{carried by the problem}, the one-sided limit $f(0^+)$ (or $f(0^-)$ in the
convention that places impulses just before $t=0$). When forcing contains a
$\delta(t)$ at the origin, the $0^-$ convention is the safe one: it captures the
impulse \emph{inside} the integral instead of losing it at the lower limit. State
your convention and use it consistently across every derivative term.

quiz.prompt: With $Y(s) = \mathcal{L}\{y(t)\}$, what is $\mathcal{L}\{y'(t)\}$?
What is the Laplace transform $\mathcal{L}\{y''(t)\}$ in terms of $Y(s)$?
The derivative theorem $\mathcal{L}\{y''\} = s^2 Y - s\,y(0) - y'(0)$ requires which pieces of data?
quiz.answer: The initial position $y(0)$ and initial velocity $y'(0)$

quiz.prompt: For $y(0) = 1$ and $y'(0) = 0$, what is $\mathcal{L}\{y''\}$ in terms of $Y(s)$?

modules.title: Inverse Transforms and Partial Fractions
modules.topics: The partial-fraction playbook for rational $Y(s)$, the four shifted-trig inversion patterns from a completed square, and how to choose an inversion route.

formulas.title: The Four Shifted-Trig Inverses
formulas.body:
Almost every irreducible-quadratic inversion reduces to one of four shifted-trig
patterns. Complete the square, $s^2+bs+c \to (s+a)^2+\omega^2$ with $a=b/2$ and
$\omega = \sqrt{c - a^2}$, rewrite the numerator in terms of $(s+a)$, and match:
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.8}
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
F(s) & f(t) \\ \hline
\dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2+\omega^2} & e^{-at}\sin\omega t \\
\dfrac{s+a}{(s+a)^2+\omega^2} & e^{-at}\cos\omega t \\
\dfrac{1}{(s+a)^2+\omega^2} & \dfrac{1}{\omega}\,e^{-at}\sin\omega t \\
\dfrac{s}{(s+a)^2+\omega^2} & e^{-at}\!\left(\cos\omega t - \dfrac{a}{\omega}\sin\omega t\right) \\
\end{array}
\]
The $(s+a)$ part becomes a $\cos$ term; the leftover constant becomes a $\sin$ term
(scaled by $1/\omega$).

formulas.title: Choosing an Inversion Route
formulas.body:
(1) \textbf{Table match} --- recognize the pair directly, after completing the
square or factoring out an $e^{-cs}$. (2) \textbf{Partial fractions} --- for any
proper rational $Y(s)=P/Q$; the workhorse. (3) \textbf{First/second shift} --- peel
off $e^{at}$ factors ($s$-shift) and delays ($e^{-cs}$, $t$-shift).
(4) \textbf{Convolution} --- when $Y=F\cdot G$ is a product with no clean
partial-fraction split. (5) \textbf{Derivative-of-transform} --- when $Y$ looks like
$-G'(s)$, read it as $t\,g(t)$.

examples.title: Worked Example --- A Repeated Root
examples.setup:
Invert $Y(s) = \dfrac{s+3}{(s+1)^2}$. A repeated factor needs two terms,
$\dfrac{A}{s+1}+\dfrac{B}{(s+1)^2}$. Clear denominators:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{s+3 = A(s+1) + B
   \;\Rightarrow\; A = 1,\; B = 2.} \]
Each term has a known inverse ($1/(s+1)\to e^{-t}$ and $1/(s+1)^2\to t e^{-t}$):
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(t) = e^{-t} + 2t\,e^{-t} = (1+2t)\,e^{-t}.} \]
The $t e^{-t}$ term --- a polynomial times an exponential --- is the fingerprint of a
repeated real root, exactly as in the homogeneous solution from undetermined
coefficients.

methodology.title: Partial-Fraction Playbook
steps:
\textbf{Irreducible quadratic} $s^2+bs+c$: \textbf{complete the square} to
$(s+\alpha)^2+\beta^2$, then match to shifted $\sin/\cos$ via the first
shift theorem.

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body:
A completed square $(s+a)^2+\omega^2$ \emph{is} the pole pair
$s=-a\pm i\omega$ written algebraically. Reading $a$ and $\omega$ off the denominator
hands you the \textcolor{accentorange}{decay rate} and the \textcolor{accentorange}{oscillation frequency} of the
answer before you finish the inversion --- the same information the pole diagram
shows geometrically.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Don't Factor a Complex-Root Quadratic
warning.body:
When $Q$ has complex roots, resist factoring it into complex linear terms. Complete
the square instead: $s^2+2s+5 = (s+1)^2+4$ inverts cleanly to $e^{-t}$ times a
$\sin/\cos$ in $2t$. Factoring into $(s-(-1+2i))(s-(-1-2i))$ is correct but invites
arithmetic mistakes a real reader will not catch.

quiz.prompt: Why are partial fractions used when inverting a rational function $F(s)$?
quiz.answer: They break $F(s)$ into simple terms whose inverse transforms are known

quiz.prompt: Find $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s^2 + 4}\right\}$.
Find $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s(s-2)}\right\}$ using partial fractions.
Find $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{1}{(s-1)^2}\right\}$.

modules.title: Solving Initial Value Problems
modules.topics: The five-step transform method, worked end to end on a distinct-root IVP and an underdamped IVP with both initial conditions nonzero.

examples.title: Worked Example --- A Full IVP
examples.setup:
Solve $y'' - 3y' + 2y = 0$, $y(0)=1$, $y'(0)=0$. Transform and substitute:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\big(s^2 Y - s\big) - 3\big(sY - 1\big) + 2Y = 0}
   \;\Rightarrow\; (s^2-3s+2)Y = s-3. \]
Solve and factor the denominator $s^2-3s+2=(s-1)(s-2)$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{Y(s) = \frac{s-3}{(s-1)(s-2)}
   = \frac{2}{s-1} - \frac{1}{s-2}.} \]
Invert each term:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(t) = 2e^{t} - e^{2t}.} \]

examples.title: Worked Example --- An Underdamped IVP, End to End
examples.setup:
Solve $y'' + 2y' + 5y = 0$, $y(0)=2$, $y'(0)=-2$. Transform with both derivative
rules and substitute the data:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\big(s^2 Y - 2s + 2\big) + 2\big(sY - 2\big) + 5Y = 0.} \]
Collect $Y$ and the constants:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{(s^2 + 2s + 5)\,Y = 2s + 2
   \;\Rightarrow\; Y(s) = \frac{2s + 2}{s^2 + 2s + 5}.} \]
Complete the square in the denominator, $s^2+2s+5 = (s+1)^2 + 4$, and align the
numerator to the shifted variable $s+1$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{Y(s) = \frac{2(s+1)}{(s+1)^2 + 2^2}.} \]
This is exactly $2$ times a shifted cosine ($a=-1$, $\omega=2$):
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(t) = 2\,e^{-t}\cos 2t.} \]
The $e^{-t}$ envelope (from the real part of the poles) and the $2t$ oscillation
(from the imaginary part) are read straight off the completed square.

methodology.title: The Five-Step Method
steps:
\textbf{Transform} both sides of the ODE, applying the derivative rules so
the initial conditions enter immediately.

\textbf{Solve algebraically} for $Y(s)$ --- it is always a rational
function (times $e^{-cs}$ factors if the forcing is delayed).

\textbf{Decompose} $Y(s)$ by partial fractions / completing the square into
table-ready pieces.

quiz.prompt: What are the three main stages of solving an IVP with Laplace transforms?
quiz.answer: Transform the equation, solve algebraically for $Y(s)$, then invert

quiz.prompt: Transform the IVP $y' - y = 0$, $y(0) = 1$. What equation for $Y(s)$ results?
Solve $y' + 2y = 0$ with $y(0) = 3$ by transforming. What is $Y(s)$?
Continuing, $Y = 3/(s+2)$. What is $y(t)$?

modules.title: Heaviside and Piecewise Functions
modules.topics: The unit step as a switch, the second shift theorem, packaging piecewise forcing into shifted form, and solving a step-forced IVP without patching across breakpoints.

formulas.title: Heaviside and the Second Shift
formulas.body:
\[ u(t-c) = \begin{cases} 0, & t<c\\ 1, & t\ge c \end{cases}, \qquad
   \mathcal{L}\{u(t-c)\} = \frac{e^{-cs}}{s}. \]
\[ \boxed{\;\mathcal{L}\{u(t-c)\,f(t-c)\} = e^{-cs} F(s)\;} \qquad
   \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{e^{-cs}F(s)\} = u(t-c)\,f(t-c). \]
A delay by $c$ in time multiplies the transform by $e^{-cs}$.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Packaging a Two-Piece Function
examples.setup:
Transform $f(t) = \begin{cases} t, & 0\le t<2 \\ 1, & t\ge 2 \end{cases}$. Write it as
a base piece plus a switched correction at $c=2$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{f(t) = t - u(t-2)\,(t-1).} \]
The correction must be expressed in $(t-2)$: since $t-1 = (t-2)+1$,
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{f(t) = t - u(t-2)\big[(t-2)+1\big].} \]
Transform term by term with $\mathcal{L}\{t\}=1/s^2$ and the second shift:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{F(s) = \frac{1}{s^2}
   - e^{-2s}\!\left(\frac{1}{s^2} + \frac{1}{s}\right).} \]

examples.title: Worked Example --- Step-Forced System
examples.setup:
Solve $y'' + y = u(t-\pi)$, $y(0)=0$, $y'(0)=0$. Transform, using
$\mathcal{L}\{u(t-\pi)\}=e^{-\pi s}/s$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{(s^2+1)Y = \frac{e^{-\pi s}}{s}
   \;\Rightarrow\; Y(s) = e^{-\pi s}\cdot\frac{1}{s(s^2+1)}.} \]
Decompose the $s$-free factor: $\dfrac{1}{s(s^2+1)} = \dfrac{1}{s} - \dfrac{s}{s^2+1}$,
which inverts to $1-\cos t$. Now apply the second shift ($t\mapsto t-\pi$, switched at
$\pi$):
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(t) = u(t-\pi)\,\big[\,1 - \cos(t-\pi)\,\big]
   = u(t-\pi)\,\big[\,1 + \cos t\,\big].} \]
The system sits still until $t=\pi$, then oscillates about its new equilibrium.

methodology.title: Packaging a Piecewise Function for the Transform
steps:
Rewrite the piecewise $f(t)$ as a sum of steps:
$f(t) = f_0(t) + \sum_k u(t-c_k)\,[\,f_{k+1}(t)-f_k(t)\,]$.

For each step, force the trailing factor into the shifted form
$g(t-c_k)$ --- i.e. \textbf{re-express every $t$ as $(t-c_k)+c_k$}.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Forgetting to Shift the Argument
warning.body:
$\mathcal{L}\{u(t-c)\,f(t)\}$ is \textbf{not} $e^{-cs}F(s)$. The theorem requires the
\emph{same} shift inside $f$. If the factor is $f(t)=t^2$ switched on at $c=3$, write
$t^2 = \big((t-3)+3\big)^2 = (t-3)^2 + 6(t-3) + 9$ first, then transform each shifted
piece. Skipping this step is the single most common Heaviside error.

quiz.prompt: How is the Heaviside step function $u(t - a)$ defined?
What is the Laplace transform of the Heaviside step $u(t - a)$?
Compute $\mathcal{L}\{u(t - 2)\}$.
Find $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{e^{-3s}}{s}\right\}$.

modules.title: The Dirac Delta and Impulse Forcing
modules.topics: The delta as a distribution, the sifting property and its transform, the delta as the derivative of the step, and the impulse response of an oscillator.

formulas.title: The Sifting Property and Its Transform
formulas.body:
\[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t-c)\,g(t)\,dt = g(c) \quad(\text{sifting}), \qquad
   \mathcal{L}\{\delta(t-c)\} = e^{-cs}, \quad \mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\}=1. \]
The delta is the derivative of the Heaviside step: $\delta(t-c) = \dfrac{d}{dt}u(t-c)$,
which is why its transform is $s$ times that of the step.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Impulse-Forced Oscillator
examples.setup:
Solve $y'' + y = \delta(t-\pi)$, $y(0)=0$, $y'(0)=0$. Transform with
$\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t-\pi)\}=e^{-\pi s}$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{(s^2+1)Y = e^{-\pi s}
   \;\Rightarrow\; Y(s) = e^{-\pi s}\cdot\frac{1}{s^2+1}.} \]
Since $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/(s^2+1)\}=\sin t$, the second shift gives
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(t) = u(t-\pi)\,\sin(t-\pi) = -u(t-\pi)\,\sin t.} \]
The mass is at rest until the kick at $t=\pi$, then oscillates freely --- a clean
picture of the impulse response switched on at the moment of the blow.

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body:
A $\delta$-forcing produces a \textcolor{accentorange}{jump in the highest-order
state}: for $y''+\dots = \delta(t-c)$ the velocity $y'$ jumps by $1$ at $t=c$ while
$y$ itself stays continuous. The response after the kick is exactly the system's
free response restarted with that jolted initial condition --- the
\textcolor{accentorange}{impulse response}.

quiz.prompt: What is the defining behavior of the Dirac delta $\delta(t)$?
quiz.answer: It is zero everywhere except at $t = 0$, with total integral equal to one

quiz.prompt: The sifting property says $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\,\delta(t - a)\,dt$ equals what?
What is the Laplace transform of $\delta(t - a)$?
What is $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\}$, the transform of an impulse at the origin?

modules.title: Convolution and Periodic Functions
modules.topics: The convolution theorem, Duhamel's principle and the impulse response, Volterra integral equations, and the transform of a periodic forcing function.

formulas.title: Convolution Theorem
formulas.body:
\[ (f * g)(t) = \int_0^t f(\tau)\,g(t-\tau)\,d\tau, \qquad
   \mathcal{L}\{f*g\} = F(s)\,G(s). \]
Convolution is commutative ($f*g=g*f$), associative, and distributes over addition.

formulas.title: Duhamel / Zero-State Response
formulas.body:
For a system with transfer function $H(s)$ and input $r(t)$ (zero initial state),
\[ Y(s) = H(s)R(s) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; y(t) = (h * r)(t)
   = \int_0^t h(t-\tau)\,r(\tau)\,d\tau, \]
where $h(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{H(s)\}$ is the impulse response --- the system's
reaction to a unit $\delta$-kick.

formulas.title: Transform of a $T$-Periodic Function
formulas.body:
If $f(t+T)=f(t)$ for $t\ge 0$, then
\[ \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = \frac{1}{1 - e^{-sT}} \int_{0}^{T} e^{-st} f(t)\,dt. \]
The factor $\dfrac{1}{1-e^{-sT}} = \sum_{n\ge 0} e^{-nsT}$ is exactly the
second-shift bookkeeping for the infinitely repeated copies of one period.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Inverting a Product by Convolution
examples.setup:
Invert $Y(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2(s^2+1)}$ by reading it as
$\dfrac{1}{s^2}\cdot\dfrac{1}{s^2+1}$, i.e. $f(t)=t$ convolved with $g(t)=\sin t$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(t) = \int_0^t \tau\,\sin(t-\tau)\,d\tau.} \]
Integrate by parts (or use the angle expansion):
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(t) = \big[\tau\cos(t-\tau)\big]_0^t
   - \int_0^t \cos(t-\tau)\,d\tau = t - \sin t.} \]
A check confirms it: partial fractions give
$\frac{1}{s^2(s^2+1)} = \frac{1}{s^2} - \frac{1}{s^2+1}$, which also inverts to
$t - \sin t$.

examples.title: Worked Example --- A Convolution-Type Volterra Equation
examples.setup:
Solve $y(t) = t + \displaystyle\int_0^t y(\tau)\sin(t-\tau)\,d\tau$. The integral is
$y * \sin t$, so transform both sides with $\mathcal{L}\{\sin t\}=\frac{1}{s^2+1}$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{Y = \frac{1}{s^2} + Y\cdot\frac{1}{s^2+1}
   \;\Rightarrow\; Y\left(1 - \frac{1}{s^2+1}\right) = \frac{1}{s^2}.} \]
Simplify $1-\frac{1}{s^2+1} = \frac{s^2}{s^2+1}$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{Y\cdot\frac{s^2}{s^2+1} = \frac{1}{s^2}
   \;\Rightarrow\; Y(s) = \frac{s^2+1}{s^4} = \frac{1}{s^2} + \frac{1}{s^4}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(t) = t + \frac{t^3}{6}.} \]

examples.title: Worked Example --- Square Wave
examples.setup:
For the square wave that is $1$ on $[0,a)$ and $0$ on $[a,2a)$ with period $T=2a$,
only the first half-period contributes to the integral:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\int_0^{2a} e^{-st}f(t)\,dt = \int_0^{a} e^{-st}\,dt
   = \frac{1-e^{-as}}{s}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\mathcal{L}\{f\} = \frac{1-e^{-as}}{s\,(1-e^{-2as})}
   = \frac{1}{s\,(1+e^{-as})},} \]
using $1-e^{-2as}=(1-e^{-as})(1+e^{-as})$.

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body:
Know the \textcolor{accentorange}{impulse response} and you know the response to
everything: steps, ramps, sinusoids, arbitrary signals --- all obtained by one
convolution. This is why $h(t)$ (equivalently $H(s)$) is treated as the complete
characterization of a linear time-invariant system.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: $\mathcal{L}\{fg\}\ne F(s)G(s)$
warning.body:
The transform of a \emph{product} is not the product of transforms. $F(s)G(s)$
inverts to the \emph{convolution} $f*g$, never to $f(t)g(t)$. Use convolution to
invert a transform that is naturally a product --- especially $Y(s)=H(s)\cdot R(s)$,
system times input.

quiz.prompt: How is the convolution $(f * g)(t)$ defined?
The convolution theorem states that $\mathcal{L}\{f * g\}$ equals what?
For a function periodic with period $T$, the Laplace transform is given by which formula?
In the periodic transform formula, what does $T$ represent?

modules.title: The Fourier Connection
modules.topics: The Laplace transform as a damped, one-sided Fourier transform, and the imaginary axis as the Fourier axis where the frequency response lives.

formulas.title: Laplace as a Damped Fourier Transform
formulas.body:
Write $s = \sigma + i\omega$. Then
\[ F(s) = \int_0^\infty e^{-(\sigma + i\omega)t} f(t)\,dt
   = \int_0^\infty \big[\,e^{-\sigma t} f(t)\,\big]\,e^{-i\omega t}\,dt. \]
This is the Fourier transform of the \emph{damped, one-sided} signal
$e^{-\sigma t}f(t)$. Setting $\sigma=0$ (evaluating on the imaginary axis $s=i\omega$)
recovers the Fourier transform itself, when it exists.

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body:
The imaginary axis $s = i\omega$ is the \textcolor{accentorange}{Fourier axis}: the
system's frequency response is just $H(s)$ evaluated there, $H(i\omega)$. The real
part $\sigma$ of $s$ is the convergence knob Fourier lacks --- it is what lets Laplace
handle growing and switched-on transients that pure Fourier analysis cannot.

quiz.prompt: How do the Fourier and Laplace transforms differ in the frequencies they use?
quiz.answer: Fourier uses purely imaginary frequencies, while Laplace uses general complex $s$

quiz.prompt: What does the Laplace transform add to the Fourier kernel that aids convergence?
Setting $s = i\omega$ in the Laplace transform recovers which transform?
In what sense is the Laplace transform a generalization of the Fourier transform?
quiz.answer: It evaluates the kernel for complex $s$, not just the imaginary axis

modules.title: Frequency Domain and Pole Diagrams
modules.topics: Transfer functions, poles as natural frequencies, stability from pole locations, resonance as a repeated pole, and the initial- and final-value theorems for steady-state analysis.

formulas.title: Transfer Function
formulas.body:
For a linear time-invariant system $a_n y^{(n)}+\cdots+a_0 y = r(t)$ at rest,
\[ H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{a_n s^n + a_{n-1}s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0}
   = \frac{1}{p(s)}, \]
where $p(s)$ is the system's characteristic polynomial. The numerator and denominator
encode, respectively, the system's zeros and poles.

formulas.title: Poles and Behavior
formulas.body:
A pole at $s = \alpha + i\beta$ contributes a mode $e^{\alpha t}\cos(\beta t)$ (or
$\sin$). Therefore:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\alpha < 0 \;(\text{left half-plane}) &\;\Rightarrow\; \text{decaying mode (stable)},\\
\alpha = 0 \;(\text{imaginary axis}) &\;\Rightarrow\; \text{sustained oscillation (marginal)},\\
\alpha > 0 \;(\text{right half-plane}) &\;\Rightarrow\; \text{growing mode (unstable)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
$\beta$ sets the oscillation frequency; $\alpha$ sets the growth or decay rate.

formulas.title: Initial- and Final-Value Theorems
formulas.body:
The endpoints of $f(t)$ can be read \emph{directly from $F(s)$}, without inverting:
\[ f(0^+) = \lim_{s\to\infty} s\,F(s), \qquad
   \lim_{t\to\infty} f(t) = \lim_{s\to 0} s\,F(s). \]

examples.title: Worked Example --- Step Response of a First-Order System
examples.setup:
Take $\tau y' + y = r(t)$ at rest, so $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{\tau s + 1}$. The unit-step
input $r(t)=u(t)$ has $R(s)=1/s$, so
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{Y(s) = H(s)R(s) = \frac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)}
   = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{\tau}{\tau s + 1}.} \]
Inverting (the second term is $\frac{1}{s + 1/\tau}$ in disguise):
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(t) = 1 - e^{-t/\tau}.} \]
The output relaxes to its steady state $1$ on the time scale $\tau$ --- the classic
exponential-approach step response of a first-order lag.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Pure Resonance
examples.setup:
Solve $y'' + \omega^2 y = \cos\omega t$, $y(0)=y'(0)=0$. Transforming:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{(s^2+\omega^2)Y = \frac{s}{s^2+\omega^2}
   \;\Rightarrow\; Y(s) = \frac{s}{(s^2+\omega^2)^2}.} \]
The \emph{repeated} pole at $s=\pm i\omega$ is the signature of resonance. Inverting
(via $\mathcal{L}\{t\sin\omega t\}$):
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(t) = \frac{t}{2\omega}\,\sin\omega t.} \]
The factor of $t$ --- a growing envelope --- is exactly what the doubled pole
predicts.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Beats (Near Resonance)
examples.setup:
Drive off-frequency: $y'' + \omega^2 y = \cos\nu t$ with $\nu\ne\omega$, at rest.
Transforming,
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{Y(s) = \frac{s}{(s^2+\omega^2)(s^2+\nu^2)}.} \]
Now the poles at $\pm i\omega$ and $\pm i\nu$ are \emph{distinct}, so partial
fractions give a difference of two bounded cosines:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(t) = \frac{\cos\nu t - \cos\omega t}{\omega^2 - \nu^2}.} \]
As $\nu\to\omega$ the two frequencies nearly cancel, producing the slow
amplitude-modulated envelope of \textbf{beats} --- and in the exact limit the
denominator $\omega^2-\nu^2\to 0$ forces the repeated pole and the $t$-growth of pure
resonance.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Steady State Without Inverting
examples.setup:
Reuse the step response $Y(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)}$. The final-value theorem
reads the long-run value straight off:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lim_{t\to\infty} y(t) = \lim_{s\to 0} s\cdot
   \frac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \lim_{s\to 0}\frac{1}{\tau s + 1} = 1,} \]
matching $1 - e^{-t/\tau}\to 1$ without ever inverting. The initial value checks too:
$\lim_{s\to\infty} sY(s) = \lim_{s\to\infty}\frac{1}{\tau s + 1} = 0 = y(0)$.

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body:
A system is \textcolor{accentorange}{BIBO stable} exactly when \emph{all} poles lie
strictly in the left half-plane. A single repeated pole on the imaginary axis is the
algebraic face of \textcolor{accentorange}{resonance}: a bounded input produces an unbounded,
$t$-growing output. Resonance does not come from a pole being ``large''; it comes
from a pole being \emph{repeated} --- always check pole \emph{multiplicity}, not just
location, before declaring a system resonant.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: The Final-Value Theorem Has Fine Print
warning.body:
The final-value theorem is valid \textbf{only when} $\lim_{t\to\infty}f(t)$ actually
exists --- equivalently, when every pole of $sF(s)$ lies strictly in the left
half-plane. Applied blindly to $F(s)=\dfrac{\omega}{s^2+\omega^2}$ it would
``predict'' $0$, yet $\sin\omega t$ has no limit. Always confirm the poles before
trusting the steady-state value.

quiz.prompt: What distinguishes the time domain from the frequency domain?
quiz.answer: The time domain describes a signal versus $t$; the frequency domain describes its content versus frequency

quiz.prompt: What is the role of a transform like Laplace or Fourier in this context?
quiz.answer: It moves a signal between the time and frequency domains

quiz.prompt: In a pole diagram, what are the poles of a transform $F(s)$?
quiz.answer: The values of $s$ where the denominator is zero

quiz.prompt: A pole located in the left half-plane (negative real part) corresponds to what time behavior?

modules.title: Deriving the Transform from First Principles
modules.topics: The transform as the continuous limit of a power series, half-integer powers and the Gamma function, Abel's integral equation, and PDEs dispatched by transforming in $t$.

formulas.title: From Power Series to the Transform
formulas.body:
The transform is the continuous limit of a power series. Replacing the discrete
$\sum a_n x^n$ with the integral $\int_0^\infty f(t)\,x^t\,dt$ and writing $x=e^{-s}$
(so $x^t=e^{-st}$) yields exactly
\[ \int_0^\infty e^{-st}f(t)\,dt. \]
The coefficient sequence $a_n$ becomes the function $f(t)$; the variable $x$ inside
the unit interval becomes the half-plane variable $s$.

formulas.title: Abel's Equation and Its Kernel
formulas.body:
Abel's equation --- the tautochrone problem of finding a curve of constant descent
time --- is a convolution against a half-power kernel:
\[ \int_0^t \frac{\phi(\tau)}{\sqrt{t-\tau}}\,d\tau = f(t), \qquad
   \mathcal{L}\!\left\{ t^{-1/2} \right\} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{s}}. \]
The left side is $\phi * t^{-1/2}$, so transforming gives
$\Phi(s)\sqrt{\pi/s} = F(s)$, hence $\Phi(s) = \sqrt{s/\pi}\,F(s)$, which inverts to a
$\tfrac12$-order derivative of $f$.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Heat Equation on a Half-Line
examples.setup:
For $u_t = k\,u_{xx}$, $x>0$, with $u(x,0)=0$, $u(0,t)=u_0$, and $u$ bounded as
$x\to\infty$, transforming gives
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{s\,U = k\,U_{xx} \;\Rightarrow\; U_{xx} - \tfrac{s}{k}U = 0.} \]
The bounded solution is $U(x,s)=A(s)e^{-\sqrt{s/k}\,x}$, and $U(0,s)=u_0/s$ fixes
$A=u_0/s$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{U(x,s) = \frac{u_0}{s}\,e^{-\sqrt{s/k}\,x}
   \;\Longrightarrow\; u(x,t) = u_0\,\operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{kt}}\right).} \]
The $e^{-\sqrt{s}\,x}$ signature is the hallmark of diffusion and inverts to the
complementary error function.

methodology.title: Laplace Method for an Evolution PDE
steps:
Apply $\mathcal{L}_t$ to the PDE. Time derivatives become algebra
($u_t \to s\,U - u(x,0)$); spatial derivatives survive as
$U_{xx}=\partial_{xx}U(x,s)$.

Solve the resulting ODE in $x$ for $U(x,s)$, applying the boundary
conditions (also transformed) and discarding solutions that blow up.

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body:
Half-integer powers of $t$ transform to half-integer powers of $s$ through
$\mathcal{L}\{t^{a}\} = \dfrac{\Gamma(a+1)}{s^{a+1}}$. This is what lets the Laplace
method dispatch the \textcolor{accentorange}{fractional-order} kernels that appear in
Abel-type and diffusion problems.

quiz.prompt: The Laplace transform can be motivated as the continuous analog of which discrete object?
quiz.answer: A power series, or generating function, $\sum a_n x^n$

quiz.prompt: In passing from a power series to the Laplace transform, the discrete sum is replaced by what?
quiz.answer: An integral over a continuous variable

quiz.prompt: In Mattuck's derivation, a power series $\sum a_n x^n$ is connected to the Laplace transform by which substitution?
With the substitution $x = e^{-s}$, the term $x^n$ becomes what?

problems: (From the definition) Using only the defining integral $\int_0^\infty e^{-st}f\,dt$, compute $\mathcal{L}\{e^{3t}\}$ and state the half-plane of convergence.
(Derivative rule on an IVP) Transform $y'' + 4y = 0$ with $y(0)=1$, $y'(0)=0$, solve for $Y(s)$, and invert to find $y(t)$.
(Full five-step IVP) Solve $y'' + 3y' + 2y = 0$ with $y(0)=1$, $y'(0)=0$ by the Laplace method.
(Heaviside shifting, forward) Find $\mathcal{L}\{u(t-3)\cos(t-3)\}$, then find $\mathcal{L}\{u(t-2)\,t^2\}$ by first re-expressing $t^2$ in $(t-2)$.
(Dirac impulse response, capstone) Solve $y'' + 4y = \delta(t-\pi)$ with $y(0)=0$, $y'(0)=0$, and describe the motion before and after the kick.
solutions: \noindent\textbf{Solution 1.}\quadMerge the two exponentials and integrate: $$ \int_0^\infty e^{-st}e^{3t}\,dt = \int_0^\infty e^{-(s-3)t}\,dt = \left[-\tfrac{1}{s-3}e^{-(s-3)t}\right]_0^\infty = \frac{1}{s-3}. $$ The boundary term at $\infty$ vanishes only when $s-3>0$, so the transform converges for $s>3$.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 3.}\quadStart from $\mathcal{L}\{\sin 3t\}=\dfrac{3}{s^2+9}$ and apply the first shift $s\mapsto s+2$: $$ \mathcal{L}\{e^{-2t}\sin 3t\} = \left.\frac{3}{s^2+9}\right|_{s\to s+2} = \frac{3}{(s+2)^2+9}. $$ The completed-square denominator $(s+2)^2+9$ is the fingerprint of a damped oscillation.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 4.}\quadTransform with $\mathcal{L}\{y''\}=s^2Y-sy(0)-y'(0)$ and substitute $y(0)=1$, $y'(0)=0$: $$ (s^2 Y - s) + 4Y = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; (s^2+4)Y = s \;\Rightarrow\; Y(s) = \frac{s}{s^2+4}. $$ This is exactly $\mathcal{L}\{\cos 2t\}$, so $$ y(t) = \cos 2t. $$

\noindent\textbf{Solution 5.}\quadWith $F(s)=\mathcal{L}\{e^{2t}\}=\dfrac{1}{s-2}$, apply $\mathcal{L}\{tf\}=-F'(s)$: $$ \mathcal{L}\{t e^{2t}\} = -\frac{d}{ds}\!\left(\frac{1}{s-2}\right) = -\left(-\frac{1}{(s-2)^2}\right) = \frac{1}{(s-2)^2}. $$ Equivalently, this is the first shift applied to $\mathcal{L}\{t\}=1/s^2$.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 6.}\quadDecompose $\dfrac{s+7}{(s-1)(s+3)} = \dfrac{A}{s-1} + \dfrac{B}{s+3}$. Clearing denominators, $s+7 = A(s+3) + B(s-1)$. Set $s=1$: $8=4A\Rightarrow A=2$. Set $s=-3$: $4=-4B\Rightarrow B=-1$: $$ \frac{s+7}{(s-1)(s+3)} = \frac{2}{s-1} - \frac{1}{s+3} \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathcal{L}^{-1} = 2e^{t} - e^{-3t}. $$

\noindent\textbf{Solution 7.}\quadComplete the square in the denominator: $s^2+4s+13 = (s+2)^2 + 9$. This matches the shifted $\sin$ with $a=-2$, $\omega=3$, and a $1/\omega$ scale: $$ \frac{1}{(s+2)^2+3^2} = \frac{1}{3}\cdot\frac{3}{(s+2)^2+3^2} \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathcal{L}^{-1} = \frac{1}{3}\,e^{-2t}\sin 3t. $$

\noindent\textbf{Solution 8.}\quadA repeated factor needs two terms, $\dfrac{3s+1}{(s+2)^2} = \dfrac{A}{s+2} + \dfrac{B}{(s+2)^2}$. Then $3s+1 = A(s+2)+B$, so $A=3$ and $B = 1-2A = -5$: $$ \frac{3}{s+2} - \frac{5}{(s+2)^2} \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathcal{L}^{-1} = 3e^{-2t} - 5t\,e^{-2t} = (3-5t)\,e^{-2t}. $$ The $t e^{-2t}$ term is the signature of the repeated real root.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 9.}\quadTransform and substitute $y(0)=1$, $y'(0)=0$: $$ (s^2 Y - s) + 3(sY - 1) + 2Y = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; (s^2+3s+2)Y = s+3. $$ Factor $s^2+3s+2=(s+1)(s+2)$ and decompose $\dfrac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{A}{s+1}+\dfrac{B}{s+2}$ with $A=2$, $B=-1$: $$ Y(s) = \frac{2}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2} \;\Longrightarrow\; y(t) = 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t}. $$

\noindent\textbf{Solution 10.}\quadThe first part is the second shift applied to $\mathcal{L}\{\cos t\}=\dfrac{s}{s^2+1}$ with $c=3$: $$ \mathcal{L}\{u(t-3)\cos(t-3)\} = e^{-3s}\,\frac{s}{s^2+1}. $$ For the second part, the argument $t^2$ must be rewritten in $(t-2)$ before the theorem applies: $t^2 = (t-2)^2 + 4(t-2) + 4$. Transform each shifted piece ($\mathcal{L}\{t^2\}=2/s^3$, $\mathcal{L}\{t\}=1/s^2$, $\mathcal{L}\{1\}=1/s$) and attach $e^{-2s}$: $$ \mathcal{L}\{u(t-2)\,t^2\} = e^{-2s}\!\left(\frac{2}{s^3} + \frac{4}{s^2} + \frac{4}{s}\right). $$

\noindent\textbf{Solution 11.}\quadThe factor $e^{-2s}$ signals a delay by $c=2$. Since $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/s^2\}=t$, the second shift gives $$ \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{e^{-2s}}{s^2}\right\} = u(t-2)\,(t-2). $$ The response is a ramp that stays flat at zero until it switches on at $t=2$.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 12.}\quadTransform with $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t-\pi)\}=e^{-\pi s}$ and zero initial data: $$ (s^2+4)Y = e^{-\pi s} \;\Rightarrow\; Y(s) = e^{-\pi s}\,\frac{1}{s^2+4}. $$ Since $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/(s^2+4)\} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2t$, the second shift gives $$ y(t) = \frac{1}{2}\,u(t-\pi)\,\sin\big(2(t-\pi)\big) = \frac{1}{2}\,u(t-\pi)\,\sin 2t. $$ The mass sits at rest until the impulse at $t=\pi$, which jolts the velocity and launches free oscillation thereafter --- the impulse response switched on at the kick.
